nodes.name: transformFunctionPointerUses
nodes.summary: Scans all instructions for Function* operands used as values (not direct callees), replaces each with PC-relative inline asm computation to avoid absolute relocations.

nodes.name: PICTransformPass.h
nodes.summary: Header declaring PICTransformPass, an LLVM Module pass that transforms global constants and function pointers into position-independent forms producing text-only binaries.

nodes.name: PICTransformPass
nodes.summary: LLVM PassInfoMixin-based Module pass with run() method and static isRequired() returning true. Declared in llvm namespace.

layers.name: Build System
layers.description: CMake build infrastructure including modules, linker scripts, platform configurations, toolchain definitions, and post-build scripts that orchestrate the cross-platform, position-independent compilation pipeline.

layers.name: Loaders
layers.description: Host-side shellcode loaders (Python and PowerShell) that inject and execute the compiled position-independent binary on target systems.

layers.name: Core
layers.description: Freestanding runtime foundation providing primitive types, memory operations, string manipulation, math utilities, encoding, binary I/O, algorithms (base64, djb2), and compiler runtime support -- all implemented without standard library dependencies.

layers.name: Kernel
layers.description: Raw syscall definitions, OS API wrappers, and platform-specific low-level system interfaces for Linux, Windows, macOS, FreeBSD, Android, iOS, Solaris, and UEFI across multiple CPU architectures.

layers.name: Platform
layers.description: OS-abstraction layer providing portable interfaces for console I/O, filesystem access, sockets, screen capture, memory allocation, and system utilities (processes, pipes, PTY, environment, datetime) with per-platform implementations.

layers.name: Lib
layers.description: High-level library layer implementing cryptography (ChaCha20, ECC, SHA-2), TLS 1.3, HTTP client, DNS resolver, WebSocket client, image processing, JPEG encoding, and generic containers -- built on top of Core and Platform.

layers.name: Beacon
layers.description: Top-level agent command loop that handles C2 communication, command dispatching, shell interaction, and screen capture -- the primary payload logic of the position-independent agent.

layers.name: Entry Point
layers.description: The freestanding entry point that bootstraps the agent, bridging from raw shellcode execution into the beacon command loop.

layers.name: Tests
layers.description: Test suites validating core types, algorithms, crypto, networking, filesystem, platform services, and other subsystems.

layers.name: Tools
layers.description: LLVM compiler pass (pic-transform) that rewrites code to ensure position-independent execution, a critical build-time transformation for shellcode generation.

tour.title: The Entry Point: Where Shellcode Begins
tour.description: Every position-independent binary starts executing at a single function. entry_point.cc is the first byte of the .text section -- verified by the build system. It bootstraps platform context (e.g., UEFI handles), calls start(), and terminates via ExitProcess. This is the only function the linker map must place first.

tour.title: Freestanding Primitives: No Standard Library
tour.description: With -nostdlib and -ffreestanding, there are no standard types. primitives.h defines every fundamental type (INT8 through UINT64, BOOL, SIZE_T, variadic macros, calling conventions) from scratch. span.h provides a zero-cost non-owning buffer view modeled after std::span, and result.h implements Rust-style Result<T,E> for error handling. These three files are the vocabulary types used everywhere.

tour.title: CRT-Free Memory and Strings
tour.description: Without libc, memcpy/memset/memmove must be implemented by hand. memory.cc provides word-at-a-time optimized versions. string.h implements a full StringUtils static class (comparison, searching, number conversion, UTF encoding) all without CRT. These replace the C runtime foundation that normal programs take for granted.

tour.title: Direct Syscalls: Talking to the Kernel
tour.description: Instead of calling libc wrappers, the agent issues raw syscalls via inline assembly. Each OS/architecture combination has its own System::Call overloads (e.g., svc #0 on AArch64, syscall on x86_64, int $0x80 on i386). The syscall.h headers define per-platform syscall numbers and kernel structures. platform_result.h converts raw return values into the Result<T,E> type.

tour.title: Windows Kernel Layer: PEB Walking and PE Parsing
tour.description: On Windows there are no syscall numbers to call directly. Instead, the agent walks the Process Environment Block (PEB) to locate loaded DLLs, parses PE export tables, and resolves API function pointers using DJB2 compile-time hashes -- a classic shellcode technique. This avoids import tables entirely.

tour.title: Platform Abstraction: Portable OS Services
tour.description: Above the raw kernel layer sits a portable abstraction: Allocator for heap memory (via mmap/VirtualAlloc/EFI), Socket for TCP, File/Directory for filesystem access, Console for output, and SystemInfo/DateTime/Process for system services. Each has per-platform implementations (posix/, windows/, uefi/) selected at compile time. This is how one codebase spans 8 operating systems.

tour.title: Custom Cryptography: SHA-2, ECC, ChaCha20-Poly1305
tour.description: TLS 1.3 requires serious cryptography, all implemented from scratch. SHA-256/SHA-384 use traits-based templates with round constants embedded as immediates (not lookup tables, which would create .rodata). ECC implements NIST P-256/P-384 with big-integer arithmetic for ECDH key exchange. ChaCha20-Poly1305 provides the AEAD cipher for TLS record encryption.

tour.title: TLS 1.3 Client: Handshake to Encrypted Records
tour.description: The TLS stack implements a full 1.3 handshake: ClientHello/ServerHello, ECDH key share, HKDF key schedule, encrypted extensions, and certificate processing. TlsCipher manages the key schedule and ChaCha20Encoder handles bidirectional record encryption. TlsBuffer provides dynamic memory for record I/O. The result is encrypted TCP without OpenSSL or any external dependency.

tour.title: DNS, HTTP, and WebSocket: The Network Stack
tour.description: DNS resolution uses DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC 1035 queries via TLS POST to Cloudflare/Google). HttpClient handles URL parsing, connection creation with IPv6-to-IPv4 fallback, and HTTP/1.1 request/response. WebSocketClient implements RFC 6455 on top of TLS: opening handshake, masked framing, fragmentation reassembly, and control frames. This is the C2 transport layer.

tour.title: The Beacon: Command Loop and Protocol
tour.description: The beacon is the top-level agent logic. main.cc::start() registers command handlers, connects via WebSocket to the relay server, and enters a receive-dispatch-respond loop with automatic reconnection. commands.h defines the binary protocol: 9 command types (system info, directory listing, file read, shell I/O, screenshots) with a CommandHandler function pointer dispatch table.

tour.title: Beacon Capabilities: Shell and Screen Capture
tour.description: The Shell class wraps platform ShellProcess (PTY on POSIX, cmd.exe pipes on Windows) to provide interactive remote shell access. Screen capture uses platform-specific screen grabbing, binary image differencing for dirty-rectangle detection, and a from-scratch baseline JPEG encoder (DCT with float constants encoded as UINT32 immediates for PIC). These are the agent's most complex capabilities.

tour.title: The PIC Transform: Making C++ Position-Independent
tour.description: The key innovation is a custom LLVM compiler pass that rewrites IR to eliminate .data, .rodata, .bss, and .got sections at compile time. Global constants, string literals, and lookup tables are transformed into code-relative constructs. Without this pass, standard C++ compilation would produce relocatable but not position-independent shellcode. The build system integrates it as either a loadable plugin or standalone tool.

tour.title: Build System: From C++23 Source to Raw Shellcode
tour.description: The CMake build pipeline orchestrates the entire compilation: freestanding toolchain (clang, no CRT), platform-specific flags and linker scripts that merge all sections into .text, the PIC transform pass, and post-build steps that extract the .text section into a raw binary, base64-encode it, and verify position-independence by checking the linker map for forbidden sections. Custom linker scripts per architecture ensure correct section merging.

tour.title: Loaders: Injecting the Shellcode
tour.description: Once compiled to a raw .text blob, the shellcode needs a loader to execute it. The Python loader auto-detects OS/architecture, downloads the correct binary from GitHub Releases, and executes via mmap+mprotect on POSIX or process injection (VirtualAllocEx, CreateRemoteThread) on Windows. The PowerShell loader does the same for Windows-only environments. These are the delivery mechanism for the position-independent agent.
